ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Burmese chicken fried noodles; A0CKP (stir-fried noodles) used as closest parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept identified for this specific Burmese noodle preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Burmese chicken fried noodles is approximately 350 g, typically served as a single-plate main course.

nutrient_source.title: Stir-fried noodles with chicken — composite estimate based on FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (egg noodles cooked, chicken breast stir-fried, vegetable oil, soy sauce, vegetables)
nutrient_source.organization: FAO/INFOODS SEA; USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Burmese chicken fried noodles exists in any approved primary source. Values estimated from weighted composite of: cooked egg noodles (~55% by weight), stir-fried chicken breast (~25%), vegetable oil (~5%), soy sauce (~5%), mixed vegetables (cabbage, bean sprouts, onion, ~10%), using FAO/INFOODS SEA and USDA SR Legacy component data. Macros cross-checked against analogous Southeast Asian stir-fried noodle dishes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of noodle-based dishes — analogous estimate based on egg noodles (GI ~50, Atkinson et al. 2008) from the University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Burmese chicken fried noodles. GI estimated at 50 based on egg noodles (GI ~50, low GI, University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283). Stir-frying with protein and fat further attenuates glycemic response. GL per serving = (50 × 19.5 × 3.50) / 100 = 34.1 g per 350 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg/100 g; ~1470 mg per serving) from soy sauce and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced soy sauce, as a standard 350 g serving provides approximately 1470 mg sodium, representing over 60% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load from egg noodles with low GI (~50) partially mitigated by protein and fat co-ingestion
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that a standard serving provides approximately 68 g of carbohydrate; the low GI (~50) and presence of protein and fat slow glucose absorption, but portion control and blood glucose monitoring remain important.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~1.2 g/100 g) and high sodium from soy sauce-based seasoning
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, the dish's sodium content is the primary concern; choosing lean chicken breast, reducing oil, and limiting soy sauce can improve the cardiovascular profile of this dish.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Burmese chicken fried noodles (ခေါက်ဆွဲကြော် ကြက်, romanized: Khao Swe Kyaw Kyat) is a popular stir-fried noodle dish from Myanmar, widely served in Burmese restaurants across Southeast Asia including Singapore. The dish consists of egg noodles wok-tossed over high heat with sliced or shredded chicken, garlic, onion, cabbage, bean sprouts, egg, and soy-based seasoning, finished with a drizzle of sesame or vegetable oil. The result is a savory, slightly smoky dish with tender noodles, juicy chicken, and crisp vegetables. Texturally, the noodles are soft yet slightly chewy, with charred edges from wok heat imparting a characteristic 'wok hei' aroma. Nutritionally, a standard 350 g restaurant serving provides approximately 567 kcal, 32 g protein, 20 g fat, and 68 g carbohydrate. The glycemic index is estimated at 50 (low), making it a moderate glycemic meal when consumed in standard portions. Sodium content is elevated (~1470 mg per serving) due to soy sauce, which is a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is a good source of B vitamins, iron, and phosphorus from the chicken and noodles. It is halal-suitable and dairy-free but contains gluten (wheat noodles), egg, and soy allergens. In clinical dietary counselling, portion control and soy sauce reduction are the primary modification strategies for patients with cardiometabolic conditions.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Burmese chicken fried noodles exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of approved component data (FAO/INFOODS SEA and USDA SR Legacy). GI is estimated from the closest analogous food (egg noodles, GI 50) from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates; no single approved source entry exists for this specific Burmese dish. Values derived from FAO/INFOODS SEA and USDA SR Legacy component data weighted by estimated ingredient proportions. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources for this dish type. 3. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g/100 g; confirmed zero based on the absence of hydrogenated fats in the standard recipe formulation. 4. GI estimated at 50 (low) based on egg noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database; stir-frying with protein and fat further attenuates glycemic response. 5. Sodium content is high due to soy sauce; clinical guidance recommends reduced-sodium soy sauce or reduced portion for hypertensive patients. 6. The cuisine tag 'Lor Mee' in the input appears to be a field survey platform tagging artefact and does not accurately describe this dish; it has been disregarded. 7. Burmese cuisine does not fall under the Singaporean multilingual naming requirement (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi); the mandatory language for this cuisine is the romanized Burmese form, which is provided in romanized_local and aliases.
